hypothesis: If tensor completion surrogate models (CPD-S and NeAT) are trained to predict Young's modulus E and mass-normalized modulus Ẽ from lattice design parameters under both uniform random and biased sampling regimes, then they will achieve higher R² scores than baseline ML methods (Gaussian Process, XGBoost, and MLP) across varying training sizes because they more effectively capture multi-way interactions among design variables.
